title: The Accusative Case (Akkusativ)
description: The accusative case is used for the direct object of a sentence — the person or thing directly affected by the action. It is also required after certain prepositions and in some time expressions without a preposition.
whenToUse: For the direct object of a verb (the thing being acted upon)
After the prepositions durch, für, gegen, ohne, um, and entlang
In time expressions without a preposition (jeden Tag, letzten Monat, etc.)
After certain verbs that always take an accusative object (haben, brauchen, sehen, etc.)

rules.title: Direct objects take the accusative
rules.explanation: The direct object is the person or thing directly receiving the action. To find it, ask "Wen?" (whom?) or "Was?" (what?) after the verb. Only the masculine article changes: der becomes den, ein becomes einen.

examples.explanation: "Den Mann" is the direct object. The masculine article changes from "der" to "den" in the accusative.
"Eine Tasche" is the direct object. Feminine articles do not change in the accusative.
"Das Buch" is the direct object. Neuter articles do not change in the accusative.

rules.title: Accusative prepositions: durch, für, gegen, ohne, um, entlang
rules.explanation: These prepositions always require the accusative case. A helpful mnemonic is "DOGFU" (durch, ohne, gegen, für, um). Note that "entlang" usually follows the noun.

examples.correct: Das Geschenk ist für den Lehrer.
examples.incorrect: Das Geschenk ist für der Lehrer.
examples.explanation: After "für", the masculine article must change to "den".
After "durch", the noun takes the accusative: "den Park".
"Entlang" follows the noun and takes the accusative: "die Straße entlang".

rules.title: Only masculine articles change in the accusative
rules.explanation: This is the key simplification of the accusative: feminine, neuter, and plural articles remain identical to the nominative. Only masculine articles change (der → den, ein → einen, kein → keinen).

examples.correct: Ich kenne den Mann. (masculine: der → den)
examples.explanation: Masculine definite article changes from "der" to "den".

examples.correct: Ich kenne die Frau. (feminine: die → die)
examples.explanation: Feminine definite article stays "die" — no change.

examples.correct: Ich kenne das Kind. (neuter: das → das)
examples.explanation: Neuter definite article stays "das" — no change.

rules.title: Time expressions without a preposition use the accusative
rules.explanation: When expressing duration or a specific point in time without a preposition, German uses the accusative case. This is common with words like jeden, letzten, nächsten, diesen.

examples.explanation: "Jeden Tag" (every day) is an accusative time expression without a preposition.

examples.correct: Sie war letzten Monat in Berlin.
examples.explanation: "Letzten Monat" (last month) uses the accusative for a time reference.

examples.correct: Wir fahren nächste Woche nach Hamburg.
examples.explanation: "Nächste Woche" (next week) — feminine stays the same, but it is still an accusative time expression.

commonMistakes.explanation: The direct object requires the accusative. Masculine "der" must change to "den".
"Ohne" takes the accusative, not the dative. Use "seinen", not "seinem".
The direct object of "haben" is accusative. Both the article and the adjective must reflect the accusative masculine ending.
"Gegen" requires the accusative, not the dative.
Time expressions without a preposition take the accusative: "jeder" (nom.) becomes "jeden" (acc.).

tips: Remember: only the MASCULINE article changes in the accusative — all others stay the same as in the nominative.
Use the question "Wen oder was?" (whom or what?) to identify the direct object.
Learn the accusative prepositions as a group: durch, für, gegen, ohne, um (+ entlang). The mnemonic "DOGFU" can help.
When in doubt about whether a verb takes accusative or dative, check if the object is directly affected by the action — if yes, it is usually accusative.
Practice with "haben" — it always takes an accusative object: Ich habe einen Hund, eine Katze, ein Auto.
